turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Problema. And the problem:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: El problema. Good. We can see it ends in an a, so we would expect it to be feminine, but we saw that these words ending - ma in Spanish, many of them entered through Greek. And in Greek they're neuter, but they look a bit masculine in Greek, so they came into Spanish as masculine words even though they end in a. So we have el problema. System:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Sistema. The system:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. The paradigm: how do you think that would be in Spanish?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: El paradigma. Good. Scheme also came in from Greek. But scheme in Spanish means outline or diagram. How do you think that would be?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good, and something else to make it sound a bit more Spanish?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Very good. El esquema. We said that we cannot start s plus another consonant in Spanish. We have to put an e before, like Spain, España, spaghetti, espagueti. El esquema, so this would be outline or diagram. Diagram is another one of these words. How do you think that would be in Spanish?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And the diagram:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: El diagrama. Good. So mostly, words ending / o / are masculine and words ending / a / are feminine, but we might find some exceptions. Planeta, for example, what do you think that means? Planeta:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Planeta is a masculine word. So the planet:

turns.speaker: tutor

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Un planeta, very good. How would you say the planets?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Very good, los planetas. Good. So this is a masculine word that looks feminine. How was the word for car? We saw various words.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Auto, let's work with that one. How do you say the car?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: The cars:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Los autos. Good. If you want to say this car, you say este auto. Este auto.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. We saw esta noche, this night. What do you think is the difference between este and esta, then? If we have este auto and esta noche, what do you think is the difference?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So, we see that noche is a feminine word. How would you say the night?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: La noche. The nights:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. But this night, or tonight:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Esta noche. Good. If you take this t out of este and esta, the words for this, you get the word for that. So este auto is this car. And that car would be?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ese auto. How would you say I want that car?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. I don't want that car, because it's very big. Big is grand so how would that be in Spanish?

turns.speaker: tutor

turns.text: And how would be that night?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Esa noche. We just get rid of the t and then we have the word for that. What if you wanted to say those nights?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Esas noches. Good. There are also a few words that end in o which are feminine. For example, mano. Mano means hand. Ends in o, but it's a feminine word. So how would you say the hand?

turns.text: La mano. Good. The hands:

turns.text: Las manos. This hand:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. That hand:

turns.text: Good. These hands:

turns.text: Good. We have this word in English, like in manuscript, manual, as well. Manual, manuscript, that man - bit there means hand. Maintain, which in Spanish is mantener. We have main - or man - means hand. Manage. Manage means to handle. Actually, it has a special use for handling horses, historically. Which is interesting, because to manage, manejar, is used in some Latin American countries to mean to drive. Other than to manage or to handle, it also means to drive, manejar, which is interesting because this verb manage historically had the special use to handle horses, and we see how that's kind of translated over the years to driving. How would you say Can you give me a hand? So this is a literal translation of something idiomatic in English, but it works in Spanish as well. In Spanish you say this. Can you give me a hand? And let's talk informally.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: But it's -

turns.text: Good. ¿ Puedes darme?, or ¿ me puedes dar?, una mano. How would you say Could you give me a hand? Could you give me a hand? So could is would can.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. ¿ Me podrías dar una mano? ¿ Podrías darme una mano? You should give me a hand. You should give me a hand. Should is would owe. Still informal.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Estoy manejando el problema. So mano, we said, ends in o and it's feminine. It's actually kind of the only true word that ends in o and is feminine. So, for example we have la foto, the foto. But foto is short for fotografía. So it's actually la fotografía becomes la foto. The same with la moto. The motorbike is actually la motocicleta. No? Then people say moto and foto and it stays with la. To take in Spanish is tomar. Tomar.

turns.text: Good. So how would you say I'm taking a photo, and you mean right now?
